schools.summary: Rehoboam succeeds to Judah but must be 'made king' by Israel, who claim a right to conditions; Shechem's choice forebodes revolution.
Henry: Solomon's one son was a fool — wisdom runs not in the blood; the people meant to unmake, not make, him, yet it was of the Lord.
Gill: Rehoboam knew of the threatened rending and hoped to forestall it at Shechem, but the move hastened the rupture.
Clarke: Rehoboam was likely Solomon's only son, a foolish heir — the fruit of unbounded matrimonial excess.
The complex Hebrew sentence runs on into v3; Chronicles and the LXX read 'returned from Egypt' where Kings has 'dwelt in Egypt.'
Gill: the people sent and called Jeroboam, making the returned fugitive their spokesman before Rehoboam.

debates.question: Was Jeroboam still in Egypt, or already returned when summoned?

sides.position: still in Egypt and only later called (the Masoretic text of Kings)
already returned from Egypt to his city, then sent for (Chronicles/LXX, more probable)

schools.summary: 'All the congregation of Israel' assembles to petition; K&D weigh Solomon's real burdens against the peace and wealth his reign secured.
Gill: chiefly Ephraimites sent for Jeroboam, whose summons may have prompted his very return from Egypt.
Ellicott: the tribes acted within their right; heavy taxation and forced labour broke old traditions and even Solomon's earlier practice.
Clarke: two real grievances — laborious service and heavy tax; late Solomon revived an Egyptian-style bondage.
Gill: the complaint was largely groundless; they lived in peace and plenty and said nothing of Solomon's idolatry, their true grievance.

debates.question: Was Israel's complaint of a 'grievous yoke' just?

sides.position: a legitimate grievance, within their rights
groundless and factious — silent about the real evil, Solomon's idolatry

schools.summary: K&D read the three-day delay as due deliberation, the king weighing the petition with his father's old counsellors.
Gill: what looks prudent was imprudent — the delay let the faction ripen and betrayed the king's unwillingness; he should have promised relief at once.
Gill: consulting the aged, experienced counsellors who had served Solomon was itself prudent — they could give the best advice.
K&D: the singular verb marks one speaking for all; the elders' counsel — serve now, be served forever — is sound, but Rehoboam forsakes it.
Clarke: the counsel states the true constitutional ideal — the king is the servant, not slave, of his people, and so their father and king.
Ellicott: it is the crafty, cynical advice of experienced politicians — 'good words' and temporary concession, yielding no substantial power.
Gill takes the elders' advice straightforwardly as the good and gracious course Rehoboam ought to have followed.

debates.question: Is the old men's counsel noble statesmanship or mere cunning?

sides.position: a true constitutional ideal of the king as servant of his people
the crafty, cynical appeasement of corrupt politicians

schools.summary: Gill: pride is the root — Rehoboam could not brook stooping to his people, so he sought counsel more congenial to his haughty temper.
K&D: he turns to the young courtiers who advise overawing threats; 'little finger' means greater might, and 'scorpions' are barbed whips.
Gill notes the king rehearsing the people's modest plea to his young circle, already bent on refusal.
K&D carry the narrative on: the king's question yields at once the young men's boast and the threat of scorpions for whips.
Clarke: reared alongside the prince, these familiar nobles counsel a proverb of pride — power as much greater than Solomon's as the thigh exceeds the little finger.
Ellicott: the spoilt children of luxurious despotism mistake obstinacy for vigour; such threats succeed only when disaffection is superficial.
Gill: they put into his mouth words that owned and magnified the charge against Solomon.
Gill: to own 'my father laid a heavy yoke' was unbecoming, conceding the charge; perhaps it reports the people's sense, not the king's admission.
Clarke: chastisement becomes punishment; the scorpion is a whip armed with iron points, or the Roman engine of poisoned darts.
Ellicott: the scorpion is a flagellum-like whip loaded with weights and sharp points.
Gill: Jeroboam returns at the head of the people, plainly now the head of the faction and their designated king, as the appointed third day arrives.
Gill: the king answered 'roughly' — blustering hard words and menaces, worked up by his young counsellors, forsaking the elders' soft answer.
Gill: Rehoboam answered 'in the very language' of the young men, owning the charge against his father and vowing to add to the yoke.
Ellicott: Scripture holds together God's all-ordaining providence and man's freedom; Rehoboam's folly worked God's judgment yet was folly still.
Gill and Henry: God left Rehoboam to his folly to fulfil Ahijah's word, serving righteous ends by the sins of men.
Clarke: the Hebrew names the 'revolution' as from the Lord, not the cruelty — God stirred revolt against an unfit king.

outliers.note: Reads sibbah as 'the Revolution/turning away' that was from the Lord, not the folly itself, and cites no ancient version supporting the common rendering.

themes: the cause was from the Lord

schools.summary: K&D: the cry echoes Sheba's revolt and voices an ancient tribal aversion to David's house, the true root of secession; Ellicott hears the sarcasm of 'see to thine own house.'
Henry: their zeal for liberty became a free people, yet they spoke unbecomingly of David and rebelled against God's anointed house — a great sin.
Gill records the withdrawal of allegiance as rebellion against their lawful prince.
Clarke: only Judah and Benjamin, reckoned as one, remained with Rehoboam.

debates.question: Was Israel's revolt righteous recoil or sinful rebellion?

sides.position: a just refusal to be trampled, becoming a free people
a great sin against David's God-established house, rooted in old tribal jealousy

themes: revolt as judgment and as sin

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott: these are ten-tribe Israelites (especially Simeon) settled in Judah's cities; the phrase 'children of Israel' hints the true Israel remained in Judah.
Gill: Israelites who had settled, or now removed, into Judah for the sake of the true worship stayed under Rehoboam.
K&D and Ellicott: Rehoboam earnestly meant to negotiate, but sending Adoram, the taskmaster of the levy (Adoniram of David's and Solomon's reigns), only inflamed the mob to stone him.
Gill: the tribute-chief was the most hateful possible envoy — another false step; too late and the wrong man.
Clarke: to send the tax-superintendent amid such ferment proved Rehoboam's folly and incapacity to govern.
Gill: it is rightly called rebellion — done by the people's own will against their lawful prince — even though it fulfilled God's purpose.
K&D mark the finality of the fall; Ellicott: 'unto this day' shows an older document embedded, the division enduring till the Assyrian captivity.
K&D: the congregation makes Jeroboam king over all Israel, Judah alone left to David; Ellicott sees his astute self-effacement and the claim to be 'the true Israel.'
Clarke: the ten tribes had no legal or constitutional right to crown Jeroboam; only God's just judgment on Solomon's house could justify it.
Gill: the common people, unaware till now of Jeroboam's return, flock to make him king.
K&D: Rehoboam raises 180,000 chosen men of Judah, Benjamin and the rest to retake the kingdom — a war soon to be forbidden as 'from the Lord.'
Gill notes the forty miles from Shechem to Jerusalem, marking the king's retreat to raise his army.
K&D and Ellicott: Shemaiah, a prophet of Judah, unexpectedly forbids Judah's war to protect the new kingdom, marking the opportunity given Israel and stressing 'your brethren.'
Gill: Shemaiah, a prophet of those days, receives 'the word of prophecy' from the Lord.

themes: the word that stops a war

schools.summary: Gill: for the first time the Lord himself names Rehoboam 'king of Judah,' ratifying the reduced realm and addressing the whole house of Judah, Benjamin, and the remnant.
Gill: the prophetic 'Thus saith the Lord' lays the separation of the tribes to God's own doing, forbidding the war.
Clarke: it was great deference to relinquish a war they thought justified; the people obeyed gladly, since the mass of mankind are averse to war.
Ellicott and K&D: 'built' means fortified; Shechem (with its patriarchal and covenant history) and Penuel become Jeroboam's east-and-west strongholds.
Henry: the fortifying was proper, but sprang from jealous fear of the people and distrust of God's promise of a 'sure house' — the root of all treacherous departures.
Gill: Jeroboam repaired and fortified Shechem and built a palace for his residence.

themes: fear of man and distrust of God

schools.summary: K&D: fearing the pull of temple worship would draw the tribes back to David's house and endanger his life, Jeroboam resolves on a rival cult — a fear 'not without reason.'
Gill: musing over his kingdom's affairs, Jeroboam conceives the scheme 'in his heart,' his own private reasoning.
K&D: Jeroboam judges, not unreasonably, that pilgrimage to Jerusalem will turn hearts back to Rehoboam, and takes counsel with the heads who made him king.
Gill: the people were bound to sacrifice at the temple three times a year and nowhere else, so the pull toward Jerusalem was constant.
Clarke: Jeroboam rightly distrusted a fickle people who followed him from caprice, not principle — but never trusted God's promise.
K&D and Ellicott: the gilded wooden calves revive Aaron's Sinai idolatry, drawing on Egyptian and Ephraimite emblems; a breach of the Second Commandment that would degenerate toward polytheism.
Clarke: a 'political religion' — religion made a state engine — with the effrontery of echoing Aaron's very words despite the judgments they recalled.
Gill: Jeroboam took counsel with men as irreligious as himself, concerned only for the civil state.
K&D: the calves broke both the ban on images and the law of the one chosen place; Ellicott: Bethel and Dan, frontier towns, carried patriarchal and old-sanctuary associations Jeroboam exploited.
Gill: Bethel was chosen partly because its very name, 'house of God,' and Jacob's visions there lent colour to the corruption.
Clarke: Solomon's earlier idolatry had prepared the people for these abominations at the land's two extremities.
Ellicott: no lost Bethel-clause; Dan is singled out because its old sanctuary and priesthood were ready for worship at once; K&D read 'even unto Dan' of the whole kingdom.
Gill: the calves became the occasion of idolatry, leading the people by degrees to worship the images as gods.
K&D: the house of high places and the shifted feast counterfeit the Jerusalem order, altered just enough to be Jeroboam's own.
Clarke: a 'temple of temples,' and priests taken indifferently from any comer, unfit and unconsecrated — though the true Levites likely refused.
Gill: a temple at Dan with altars for burnt offering and incense, in imitation of Jerusalem.
Ellicott: the feast mimics Tabernacles but shifts the date 'of his own heart,' and Jeroboam usurps the priest's office of sacrifice and incense, imitating and outdoing Solomon.
Clarke: meeting the people's prejudices, Jeroboam appeared to hold the thing while subverting the ordinance.
Gill: an arbitrary change, though Abarbinel thought the later harvest in the north made the eighth month fitter for an ingathering feast.

themes: a feast of his own devising

schools.summary: Ellicott: the repeated verse opens the next chapter's story; read as continuous ('was offering'), it fixes Jeroboam mid-sacrifice for the man of God to confront. K&D stress 'devised of his own heart.'
Clarke: Jeroboam acted as his own high priest, condensing civil and ecclesiastical power in his single person.
Gill: he did at Bethel as he had done in Dan, completing the apostasy at both shrines.
